items.title: Mayor Weston Wamp and Commissioner Lee Helton announced a proposal to purchase approximately 8.5 acres in Apison to pres...
items.content_text:
Mayor Weston Wamp and Commissioner Lee Helton announced a proposal to purchase approximately 8.5 acres in Apison to preserve green space and expand opportunities for parks, recreation, and youth sports.
 As one of Hamilton County’s fastest-growing communities, investing in parks today helps ensure families have places to gather, play, and enjoy the outdoors for generations to come.
 The proposal will be considered by the Hamilton County Commission, with a vote anticipated on August 5.

items.title: We have a sneak peek behind the $75 million restoration and expansion of the @tivolichatt — bringing it back to its 1920...
items.content_text:
We have a sneak peek behind the $75 million restoration and expansion of the @tivolichatt — bringing it back to its 1920s glory (with modern upgrades)!
 The entrance and lobby will feature a new box office and guest services area, a bar, new restrooms, an elevator, and a connection point to the new Tivoli Performing Arts Center being built next door. Craftsmen are currently finishing refurbishing plasterwork and leaf detailing before repainting, and all new carpet will be put in that matches the original as well. The stage area will be restored and feature all new seats.
 Next door, the center will have a Patrons Lounge, a year-round restaurant, and the Bobby Stone Theatre showing independent films and acting as a flex event space. The fully accessible space will be connected to the Tivoli at every floor, leading to the balcony area — accompanied by more guest amenities. This building will also house a rooftop bar. 
 The Tivoli is on track to reopen in October with its first show already on the books. The performing arts center is projected to be completed by the end of the year with programming slated for early 2027. Head to @tivolichatt’s website to see more about this restoration project that’s been years in the making!

items.title: Non-compete clauses are widespread across OECD countries and have a negative impact on the growth of workers’ wages. Acr...
items.content_text:
Non-compete clauses are widespread across OECD countries and have a negative impact on the growth of workers’ wages.
 Across 15 OECD countries, 30% of employees are bound by non-compete clauses, contractual terms that prevent workers from joining or starting competing firms. Such clauses reduce both wage and productivity growth.
 Stronger rules alone may not be enough to prevent the misuse of such clauses, as unclear or overly broad terms are still common.
 Governments can improve transparency, simplify regulations and step up enforcement by public authorities, including sanctions for clauses that are too broad. Social partners can also help raise awareness and ensure fair, appropriate use of these clauses. 
 Learn more how non-compete clauses impact on wages and productivity via the link in bio.

items.content_text:
Now Accepting Bids in #EastRidgeTN! 
 Springvale Park Redevelopment Project 🐾
 The City of East Ridge is now accepting sealed bids for the Springvale Park Redevelopment Project. This project will transform Springvale Park with new amenities and infrastructure designed to better serve residents and their four-legged companions.
 Project Scope Includes:
 ✔️Site grading and drainage improvements
 ✔️Paving, sidewalks, and amenity pads
 ✔️ Fencing
 🚰 Utility improvements
 🚻 Restroom facilities
 🌳 Landscaping
 The project also includes three (3) add alternate bid items as identified in the Bid Documents.
 🗓️ Pre-Bid Conference
 Monday, July 20, 2026 | 10:00 AM (ET)
 East Ridge City Hall
 1517 Tombras Avenue
 A site visit will immediately follow. Attendance is encouraged.
 🗓️ Bid Opening
 Wednesday, August 5, 2026 | 2:30 PM (ET)
 East Ridge City Hall
 1517 Tombras Avenue
 📌 Bid Submission
 Sealed bids must be delivered to:
 City of East Ridge
 ATTN: City Clerk's Office
 1517 Tombras Avenue
 East Ridge, TN 37412
 Clearly label the envelope:
 "Springvale Redevelopment Project – East Ridge"
 Bids must be received no later than 2:30 PM (ET) on Wednesday, August 5, 2026.
 📄 Project Plans & Bid Documents
 To obtain the project plans and bid book, contact Christy MacKenzie at cmackenzie@asaengineeringinc.com. All requests for plans and bid documents must be made through Asa Engineering & Consulting, Inc. to ensure receipt of future addenda and project updates.
 Questions❓
 All bidder questions and Requests for Information (RFIs) must be submitted in writing to ASA Engineering & Consulting, Inc. in accordance with the Bid Documents.
 We look forward to receiving your bid!

items.title: Tennessee's New Drink Spiking Law is Now in Effect As of July 1, 2026, intentionally spiking someone’s drink, food, or m...
items.content_text:
Tennessee's New Drink Spiking Law is Now in Effect
 As of July 1, 2026, intentionally spiking someone’s drink, food, or medication to cause involuntary intoxication is a felony in Tennessee.
 We’ve received questions from bars, restaurants, and community partners about what this new law means. One of the biggest misconceptions? The law does not require alcohol-serving establishments to purchase or provide drink testing devices. That requirement was removed before the bill became law.
 Preventing drink spiking requires a comprehensive approach—including staff training, active bystander intervention, monitoring unattended drinks, and creating a culture of safety.
 If you have questions about the law, drink testing devices, or prevention strategies, we’re here to help.
 📍 Sexual Assault Center
 Atarah A.M. Smith, MS
 Community Relations Manager
 📧 ataraham@sacenter.org
 📞 615-258-5867
 📍 Tennessee Coalition to End Domestic & Sexual Violence
 Brandon Woosley

 Together, we can help create safer bars, restaurants, and communities across Tennessee.

items.title: CPD and HCSO Share Joint Safety Message Regarding E-Bikes CHATTANOOGA, Tenn. -- The Chattanooga Police Department (CPD)...
items.content_text:
CPD and HCSO Share Joint Safety Message Regarding E-Bikes
 CHATTANOOGA, Tenn. -- The Chattanooga Police Department (CPD) and Hamilton County Sheriff's Office (HCSO) are sharing information regarding E-Bike rules and regulations.
 We know these vehicles are growing in popularity, and many people may not be aware of the legal requirements for operating them. CPD and HCSO are sharing this information now to help keep everyone safe and informed.
 Under Tennessee law, an electric bicycle must generally have fully operable pedals, two or three wheels, and an electric motor of less than 750 watts. E-Bikes must follow all rules of the road when riding on public roads and highways. However, they are not subject to laws or resolutions regarding motor vehicles, such as licensing, registration, and insurance. 
 Electric bicycles are divided into three classes. Here's the differences between each class and their limitations:
 Class 1: Pedal-assist only, with assistance ending at 20 mph. Can ride on any road or path where bicycles are allowed. Cannot ride on sidewalks unless the motor is disabled AND bicycles are allowed there.
 Class 2: Pedal-assist only, with assistance ending at 20 mph. Can ride on any road or path where bicycles are allowed. Cannot ride on sidewalks unless the motor is disabled AND bicycles are allowed there.
 Class 3:  Pedal-assist only, with assistance ending at 28 mph. Can carry a passenger if the vehicle is equipped to do so and the passenger is under the age of 14. Cannot ride on any paths or trails where bicycles are allowed, ride without a speedometer, or be operated without a helmet.
 As of July 1, 2026, Class 3 E-Bike operators must be at least 16-years-old. Other rules of the road for E-Bikes include following all traffic control devices, riding on the right-hand side of the road, using hand signals to communicate, and being equipped with a front white light and a red light or reflector visible for 500 feet. You can learn more at: https://www.tn.gov/…/bicycle-and-pedestrian-program/resourc… 
 We want everyone to enjoy these vehicles, but they must be operated legally, safely, and responsibly. CPD and HCSO will take enforcement action when vehicles are operated unlawfully or in a way that creates a safety risk. Thank you for helping keep our roadways safe. 
 #CPDnews

items.title: The United Kingdom is facing a challenging economic environment. Delivering on the government's reform agenda and mainta...
items.content_text:
The United Kingdom is facing a challenging economic environment. Delivering on the government's reform agenda and maintaining the momentum of reform can help boost productivity, support stronger growth and raise living standards.
 High and volatile energy prices, rising fiscal pressures, low investment and large regional disparities continue to weigh on the economy.
 The #OECD Economic Survey of the United Kingdom identifies reform priorities: 
 ➡️ achieving fiscal sustainability 
 ➡️ ensuring sustainability and adequacy in the pension system 
 ➡️ strengthening energy security 
 ➡️ boosting productivity across regions 
 Today, Åsa Johansson, Director of the Economics department, launched the #OECD Economic Survey of the United Kingdom alongside Torsten Bell MP, Minister for Pensions: https://brnw.ch/21x48Y3

items.title: Honored to represent Hamilton County today for a discussion about infrastructure with my friend Knox County Mayor Glenn...
items.content_text:
Honored to represent Hamilton County today for a discussion about infrastructure with my friend Knox County Mayor Glenn Jacobs, Nashville Mayor Freddie O’Connell, and Memphis Mayor Paul Young. 
 I shared about how we are trying to take a new approach to dealing with growth, preserving and protecting the beauty of rural Hamilton County, while advocating for restorative growth in blighted urban areas, and taking a tougher negotiating position with developers. The group enjoyed hearing about how Gov. Lee and Sen. Watson helped me chart a new course for McDonald Farm, turning it into the newest state park instead of an industrial park.

items.content_text:
As artificial intelligence becomes a bigger part of day-to-day work across industries, the UTC Center for Professional Education will offer two online programs in September to help working professionals build practical AI skills for different needs and experience levels.
 The two offerings are AI Essentials with Prompting, a live online workshop scheduled for Sept. 15 and Sept. 17, and CompTIA Security AI+ Certification Prep, an eight-week live online course that begins Sept. 19. Together, the programs give learners two entry points into AI-related professional development: one focused on broad workplace fluency, productivity and responsible use, and one designed for cybersecurity professionals working in a threat environment increasingly shaped by AI.
 🔗  Read more here: https://blog.utc.edu/…/utc-center-for-professional-educati…/

items.title: It’s official: This Friday, July 17th is Parks and Outdoors Day in Chattanooga!  The City Council passed the resolution...
items.content_text:
It’s official: This Friday, July 17th is Parks and Outdoors Day in Chattanooga! 
 The City Council passed the resolution this afternoon, and we are so grateful to see our team's hard work recognized. From maintaining our neighborhood playgrounds to engaging our youth with programs and camps, our team is out there making sure Chattanooga stays beautiful, safe, and fun for everyone.
 Pictured here is our new Administrator, David Johnson, speaking at the City Council meeting today to advocate for our staff and accept this special recognition on behalf of the whole department. We hope you'll get outside this Friday and enjoy the incredible nature our city has to offer (help us hope for good weather!).
 We want to hear from you: What’s your absolute favorite park, trail, or outdoor spot in Chattanooga? Know a member of our staff who went above and beyond for you? Have a favorite pollinator garden or playground? Let us know in the comments!

items.title: The Health Department’s Radiological Response Team recently participated in two full-scale exercises to demonstrate our...
items.content_text:
The Health Department’s Radiological Response Team recently participated in two full-scale exercises to demonstrate our readiness to respond during a nuclear power emergency.
 Working alongside local, state, and national partners, the team practiced setting up a community reception center, monitoring individuals, providing decontamination support, and distributing potassium iodide when necessary.
 These exercises help to ensure our team is trained, equipped, and prepared to protect the health of our community.
 We want to thank our partners with the Hamilton County Emergency Management Agency, local schools, and all other partners who helped make this exercise possible.

items.title: Instead of asking whether one vote matters, it may help to ask a different question: Who is showing up? Many Tennesseans...
items.content_text:
Instead of asking whether one vote matters, it may help to ask a different question: Who is showing up?
 Many Tennesseans feel like their vote won't make a difference. That's understandable in a state where statewide outcomes can seem predictable, but local elections often come down to who participates, not just who is registered.
 When turnout is low, a small portion of registered voters end up choosing the people who make decisions about budgets, schools, development, public safety and other local priorities.
 But participation isn't limited to Election Day! Whether you have 15 minutes or 15 hours, there are many ways to help strengthen civic participation and show up for one another (:
 Help us get out the vote this election, sign up for one of our civic actions at the link in our bio!

items.title: TN Reconnect has empowered me to pursue a wonderful goal and has given me a second chance at an education I once thought...
items.content_text:
TN Reconnect has empowered me to pursue a wonderful goal and has given me a second chance at an education I once thought I would never have. 
 My name is Darien, and I am currently pursuing a degree in cybersecurity. 
 Growing up, I didn’t have access to the kind of consistent schooling that many people take for granted, and for a long time, earning a college degree felt completely out of reach.
 The TN Reconnect program changed that for me. It offered not only financial support, but also something far more meaningful —an opportunity. For the first time, I was able to envision myself achieving a higher education and building a future in a field I’m passionate about. TN Reconnect didn’t just open a door; it gave me the means and the confidence to walk through it.
 To future TN Reconnect students, trust yourself, stay patient, and use every resource available to you. Your journey may not look like anyone else’s, and that’s okay. Take each class one step at a time, ask for help when you need it, and remember that it’s never too late to build the life you want. TN Reconnect exists to support people exactly like us — people with dreams, determination, and a desire for something better.
 I am deeply grateful for this program. It truly changes lives, and I am proud to be one of the many Tennesseans who have been able to step forward because of it.
 Learn how TN Reconnect can open doors for you at chattanoogastate.edu/tnreconnect

items.title: Not every stomach bug is the same - knowing the signs can help protect you and your family. Cyclosporiasis is a rare int...
items.content_text:
Not every stomach bug is the same - knowing the signs can help protect you and your family.
 Cyclosporiasis is a rare intestinal illness that can be transmitted through contaminated food or water. According to the CDC, cases of cyclosporiasis have been reported in multiple states across the country, including Tennessee. Although most people recover, symptoms such as diarrhea, stomach cramps, and nausea can last for days or even weeks. 
 To protect yourself, make sure to wash your hands regularly, thoroughly rinse fresh produce, and practice safe food handling. If you are experiencing persistent gastrointestinal symptoms, please contact your healthcare provider. For more information, visit https://www.cdc.gov/cyclosporiasis/index.html & refer to the graphic below.

items.title: Exploring Downtown: The Carnegie Building The Carnegie Building is one of Chattanooga's most storied landmarks. Original...
items.content_text:
Exploring Downtown: The Carnegie Building
 The Carnegie Building is one of Chattanooga's most storied landmarks. Originally built as the Carnegie Library, it is one of more than 2,000 library buildings funded by Scottish-American industrialist and philanthropist Andrew Carnegie. Having left school at age 12, Carnegie credited much of his success to the education he gained through libraries. 
 Construction on Chattanooga's Carnegie Library began in 1903, and the building officially opened to the public in 1905. Although W. T. Bowling, an Atlanta-based architect, was originally hired to design the library, Chattanooga’s most influential architect, R. H. Hunt, was appointed to complete the project after Bowling was discharged. 
 On its opening day, the library and its head librarian, Miss Margaret Dunlap, welcomed around 1,500 people. Guests were greeted not only by the library's impressive collection of books, but also by an orchestra. 
 Today, there are far fewer books in the Carnegie Building than there were a century ago, but its legacy remains closely tied to a movement that expanded access to public libraries in Chattanooga and around the world.

ICYMI: Operation Southern Slow Down is underway! 
 For the next week, CPD and law enforcement agencies across the Southeast are cracking down on speeding and aggressive driving. According to the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA), nearly 30% of deadly crashes in 2024 involved speeding. The goal of Operation Southern Slow Down is to lower that statistic by reminding drivers of the dangers of speeding and reckless driving. So please, slow down, give other drivers plenty of space, and always wear your seatbelt.

items.title: Extreme Heat and Urban Resilience in Tampa   Is extreme heat still an overlooked urban risk? Twice a week in the lead up to Gradua...
items.content_text:
Extreme Heat and Urban Resilience in Tampa
 
Is extreme heat still an overlooked urban risk? 

Twice a week in the lead up to Graduation we’re showcasing the consultancy projects, the final output undertaken by participants on LSE Cities' Executive MSc in Cities programme. Kevin O., Class of 2024-26, examines Tampa’s resilience to extreme heat—using a timely and personal lens. In July 2025, the city recorded temperatures of 100°F for the first time, highlighting the urgency of an issue often taken for granted in tropical climates. 

Working with the City of Tampa, the project stress-tested the city’s existing strategy through methods co-created with over 30 local participants. A key insight was the importance of bridging knowledge gaps—not through technical expertise alone, but by grounding conversations in lived experience. After all, weather is something everyone experiences daily, making it a powerful entry point for shared understanding. 

The work underscores the value of co-creation in shaping urban responses, particularly in cities with complex and distributed decision-making structures. Moving from strategy to implementation requires not only technical solutions, but sustained relationships, collaboration, and trust. More broadly, the project highlights a critical lesson: building resilience is as much about processes and institutions as it is about infrastructure. Creating space for dialogue, reflection, and even changing one’s mind is essential in navigating today’s urban challenges. 

This reflection also speaks to a core strength of the programme—equipping students with practical tools to turn complex, systemic issues into actionable strategies. 

Read more here: https://lnkd.in/eTfdksp8

#LSECities #ExecutiveMscInCities

items.title: Kimele Carter, executive director of the Center for Student Accessibility (CFSA) at UTC, brings decades of higher educat...
items.content_text:
Kimele Carter, executive director of the Center for Student Accessibility (CFSA) at UTC, brings decades of higher education experience to her role. A self-described lifelong campus person, Carter grew up around college campuses, began her career in academic advising and later found her calling in accessibility services.
 Carter’s role involves helping students connect with accommodations, assistive technology, guidance and support. She wants students to know the space is welcoming, not intimidating, and that any student can stop by with questions. 
 🔗  Read more here: https://blog.utc.edu/…/the-people-of-utc-get-to-know-kimel…/

items.title: Dee Dee, MeChelle, Scott, and Erica - you went Above and Beyond the Call of Duty and we could not be more grateful for e...
items.content_text:
Dee Dee, MeChelle, Scott, and Erica - you went Above and Beyond the Call of Duty and we could not be more grateful for each of you. That's why you're all recipients of the ABCD award!
 While visiting the Carousel, a family had a purse stolen from their stroller. Instead of simply passing them off to security or promising to keep an eye out, this crew spent hours assisting the family. They helped search the park, recovered most of the scattered belongings, and stayed with them while they spoke with the police. 
 The family, visiting from Murfreesboro, was so grateful that they decided to stay and finish their vacation as planned instead of packing up and going home. They could not have been happier with the level of care they received.
 Congratulations to our winners! Thank you all for your hard work this summer. You represent the very best of our department.

 The City of East Ridge was proud to celebrate the grand opening and ribbon cutting of Bright Family Dentistry on Thursday, July 9, as we officially welcomed one of our newest healthcare providers to the community network. ✂️👏🏼
 Led by Dr. Robina Habib, Bright Family Dentistry offers comprehensive dental care for patients of all ages, including preventive, restorative, cosmetic, and family dentistry services.  📍1421 Armour Street, East Ridge, TN 
 A special thank you to the East Ridge Council of the Chattanooga Chamber of Commerce, community partners, elected officials, and everyone who joined us in celebrating this exciting milestone. 
 We are excited to have them as part of the East Ridge business community and wish them many years of success serving our residents and families! 🤍

items.content_text:
🌟 Above & Beyond the Call of Duty! 🌟
 We want to give a shoutout and a well-deserved congratulations to two of our incredible team members, Marcie and Nicole!
 Recently, a mother riding a bicycle with her small child in a bike seat stopped by the Outdoor Chattanooga building. They were stranded and desperately needed an urgent bike repair.
 Without a second thought, Marcie and Nicole jumped right into action. 🛠️✨
 In less than 10 minutes, the bike was fixed, the safety checks were done, and this sweet family was safely back on their way!
 This kind of immediate, helpful, and compassionate response is exactly what makes our Parks and Outdoors Department so special. We don’t just care about the parks and trails—we care about the community members who use them.
 Thank you, Marcie and Nicole, for your quick thinking, expert skills, and for always putting our community first!
 Drop a 👏 in the comments to help us congratulate them!
 #OutdoorChattanooga #Chattanooga #CommunityFirst #GoingAboveAndBeyond #cityinapark

Thanks to funding through the National Endowment for the Arts, we have been able to support 11 projects through the ArtsBuild United We Create: America 250 grant program.
 The United We Create: America 250 grant program is providing project funding to nonprofit organizations partnering with an artist or group of artists to create artworks that elevate, honor, and reflect on the 250th anniversary of the United States. Projects engage residents and visitors in meaningful exploration of our nation’s past, present, and future, with a particular focus on the unique character and stories of Chattanooga and Hamilton County.
 Join us at Plant Bar on Tuesday, 7/14 6:30 pm for Cast Iron Storytelling’s Volunteer: True Stories from TN! Free to attend. 
 To stay up to date with all the events happening from now until the end of this year, sign up for the ArtsBuild newsletter through our website. ArtsBuild.com.
 #nationalendowmentforthearts #America250 #artsareessential #castironstorytelling #nextexitproductions

items.title: Breastfeeding can come with many challenges for a new mom, but it also offers many amazing benefits for you and your bab...
items.content_text:
Breastfeeding can come with many challenges for a new mom, but it also offers many amazing benefits for you and your baby.
 Breastfeeding is very convenient, as the milk is always warm and ready when your baby is hungry. 
 Breastfeeding helps you to save on other health and nutrition-related costs. It is very easy for babies to digest, as it is packed with essential nutrients and vitamins for strong, healthy growth. Plus, it lowers their overall risk of some illnesses, such as SIDS. 
 Need help getting started? WIC offers breastfeeding peer counselors to assist you in your breastfeeding journey. For more information regarding WIC, its programs, or your eligibility, please visit Health.HamiltonTN.org or call 423-209-8050.

This is an excellent illustration of environmental stewardship and civic commitment.  Our emergency services crew, led b...
items.content_text: This is an excellent illustration of environmental stewardship and civic commitment.  Our emergency services crew, led by Sherman Sales, has responded to the several trees that have fallen in our neighborhood due to the recent storms.  Our team not only cleared fallen debris from property and roads, but they also saved a honey bee colony, one of nature's most important pollinators.  In order to securely remove and relocate the active hive, Sherman contacted a local honey bee expert and instructed workers to set the tree aside right away. Thousands of honey bees were saved and given a new home where they can thrive because of his quick thinking and leadership.  Thank you to our fantastic crew! Pic and information provided by David Collins.

Countries are increasingly working together to tackle cross-border bribery. This shift is helping to reshape the enforce...
items.content_text:
Countries are increasingly working together to tackle cross-border bribery. This shift is helping to reshape the enforcement of anti-bribery laws worldwide.
 Multijurisdictional resolutions – where two or more countries co-ordinate their investigations and sanctions to address the same bribery scheme – are strengthening global anti-corruption efforts and helping to create a more level playing field for business.
 Authorities are increasingly co-ordinating investigations, aligning sanctions and sharing monetary recoveries across borders.
 Key insights from our latest blog post:
 ➡️ An estimated USD 33.7 billion in sanctions have been imposed through co-ordinated cases
 ➡️ An estimated USD 12.6 billion has been returned to affected jurisdictions whose officials were bribed
 This trend builds on the foundations of the OECD Anti-Bribery Convention, which has set the global standard for combating transnational corruption since 1999.
 Multijurisdictional resolutions offer a path to reshaping how the OECD Anti-Bribery Convention is implemented, replacing isolated national efforts with collaborative enforcement.
 Read the blog post to learn more: https://brnw.ch/21x45Q7
 #OECDintegrity

items.title: 🏡✨ Community Resource Hubs are coming! ✨🏡 Last week, I had the pleasure of bringing the welcome and attending the Comm...
items.content_text:
🏡✨ Community Resource Hubs are coming! ✨🏡
 Last week, I had the pleasure of bringing the welcome and attending the Community Resource Hub Partnership Breakfast at the East Lake Community Center. It was exciting to learn more about this long-awaited initiative and hear how community partners can join the City of Chattanooga in delivering programming directly within our neighborhoods.
 Community Resource Hubs are different by design—transforming our community centers into vibrant neighborhood anchors where residents can access resources, build relationships, and thrive.
 Each of the six hubs will provide coordinated programming and services in four key areas:
 💚 Health & Wellness
 📚 Education & Workforce Development
 🤝 Community Services & Social Support
 🎉 Recreation & Quality of Life
 These hubs will serve as trusted entry points to city services, centers for prevention, and spaces where meaningful partnerships create lasting impact for our communities.
 As the Chair of Community Development and Councilwoman for District 7, I'm excited to see this vision take shape and look forward to the East Lake Community Center becoming a place where opportunity, connection, and support are available closer to home.
 Thank you to everyone working to make this initiative a reality. Together, we're building stronger, healthier, and more connected neighborhoods. 💙
 📸Photos courtesy of Chris Bown
 #District7 #BuildingStrongerCommunities #CommunityResourceHubs #Chattanooga #CommunityPartnership #Health #Education #WorkforceDevelopment #CommunitySupport

items.title: This week marks the 10th residence week of the Urban95 Academy and ten city teams from across the globe are joining us in London f...
items.content_text:
This week marks the 10th residence week of the Urban95 Academy and ten city teams from across the globe are joining us in London for a week of learning engaging and sharing:
 
City of Canning, Australia
Brasilia, Brazil
Envigado, Colombia
City of Oshawa, Canada
London Borough of Camden, UK
Ndola City Council, Zambia
Sarajevo, Bosnia and Herzegovina
1803 Fund, Portland, Oregon, US
Greater London Authority, UK
 
Each team has arrived in London with an urgent, place-specific challenge at the intersection of urban design and early childhood development to address: 
 
- How to develop the city centre to be livable for families
- Ways of bringing more access to nature into school yards
- Policies and programmes to improve behavioural and emotional development in children
- Delivering safe routes for strollers
- Achieving safe and accessible doorstep play near housing
- Ensuring high-density areas allow for all children to have access to services
- Closing the learning and achievement gap for low-income children
- Expand access to and increase the use of child‑friendly public spaces among families with young children
- Strategies to mitigate impact of extreme heat on children in cities
- Strategies to support caregivers who bring children to work in market stalls where there is a lack of affordable childcare 
 
What do they all have in common? The recognition that the urban environments we design have the potential to shape the first years of life.

Stay tuned for more insights from U95 10th Residence week!

Real wages have been growing, but at a slower pace than a year ago.
 Annual real wage growth was 2.2% in Q1 2026 on average across countries, compared with 2.7% in Q1 2025.
 Yet even before the recent energy price surge, real wages in several countries had not fully recovered from the 2022 inflation spike.
 Learn more in OECD #EmploymentOutlook: https://brnw.ch/21x45fj

items.title: Our Amazing Signal Mountain Fire DepartmentYesterday, the Signal Mountain Fire Department had the opportunity to train a...
items.content_text:
Our Amazing Signal Mountain Fire Department
Yesterday, the Signal Mountain Fire Department had the opportunity to train alongside the Hamilton County Cave & Cliff Team at Signal Point Park, an area where our crews frequently respond to. 
 While this was their training event, our firefighters worked side by side with their team, sharing knowledge and experience in patient extrication from some of the most challenging terrain in our community. Together, we practiced multiple extrication routes and techniques to move injured patients safely and efficiently through the woods.
 Training together strengthens our partnerships, sharpens our skills, and ensures we’re prepared to provide the best possible care when every second counts. Thank you to the Hamilton County Cave & Cliff Team for the invitation and the opportunity to learn together!

items.title: Pedestrian Hit and Killed on Lee Highway CHATTANOOGA, Tenn. -- The Chattanooga Police Department (CPD) is investigating...
items.content_text:
Pedestrian Hit and Killed on Lee Highway
 CHATTANOOGA, Tenn. -- The Chattanooga Police Department (CPD) is investigating a crash that left a pedestrian dead. 
 CPD's Traffic Unit responded to the 6200 block of Lee Highway shortly after 11:00 Saturday night. The preliminary investigation indicates the pedestrian, a 38-year-old male, was crossing Lee Highway when a vehicle traveling East hit him. The driver stopped and remained at the scene. Hamilton County EMS transported the victim to the hospital where he was pronounced dead. 
 This information is preliminary and is subject to change as the investigation continues. Chattanooga Police ask anyone with information regarding this incident to call 423-698-2525.
 #CPDnews

ArtsBuild's Periscope CHA: Artist Entrepreneur Training program is now accepting applicants for 2027! 
 Periscope CHA empowers artist entrepreneurs through an intensive 10-week curriculum and 6 months of mentorship designed to help artists of all disciplines transform their creative passions into thriving businesses. The program culminates in a Pitch Night and Entrepreneur Expo, sponsored by Tennessee Valley Federal Credit Union, where artists will have a chance to win cash prizes of up to $10,000.
 Applications are open through August 7, 2026! Learn more at virtual and in-person info sessions on July 21: https://artsbuild.com/periscope 
 Photo Credit: ArtsBuild
